content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

March 29, 2017

Josh Fryday
Councilman
City of Novato
38 Arnold Drive
Novato, CA 94949

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-17-057

Dear Mr. Fryday:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding your duties as a Novato City
Councilmember under the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of
interest or Section 1090.

QUESTION

Do you have a conflict of interest based either on your interest in real property located
approximately 2,400 feet from the Hamilton Square 31-unit residential condominium project (the
"project") or based on the income of your spouse from Novato Charter School which may be
affected by the proposed project?

CONCLUSION

You do not have a conflict of interest in decision regarding the project. The decisions will
not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your property (as discussed below).

FACTS

Hamilton Square is a 31-unit residential condominium project proposed for property located
at 970 C Street in Novato in the Hamilton Field area. The project is located approximately 2,400
feet from your property. The subject property is surrounded by the following properties and uses:

_________________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-057
Page No. 2

• North: Parcel 1A; Public Benefit Conveyance Parcel; Novato Unified School District;
Vacant

• South: Meadow Park Residential Condominiums/Hamilton Elementary School

• East: North Bay Children's Center/Novato Charter School

• West: Lanham Village Residential Condominiums

The entitlements being sought by the property owner for the subject property are as follows:

• Use Permit: Permitting the excavation and disposal of 3,500 cubic yards of contaminated
soil. The Use Permit is being sought prior to consideration of the condominium
proposal.

• General Plan Amendment: To amend the subject property's land use designation from
Neighborhood Commercial (CN) to Medium Density Multiple Family Residential (R10)
– 10.1 to 20.00 dwelling units per acre.

• Master Plan Amendment: To amend the Hamilton Reuse Plan/Master Plan to permit 31
multi-family residential units on the subject property and increase the maximum height
limit from 30-feet to 34-feet.

• Precise Development Plan Amendment/Design Review: Addressing the physical design
of the proposed project, including vehicle/pedestrian circulation, parking,
landscaping/outdoor use spaces, and architecture.

The project is currently subject to a deed restriction imposed by the federal government
which prohibits certain uses including: residential use, a hospital for humans, a school for persons
under 21 years of age, and/or a day care center for children. The deed restriction may be modified
or terminated by the California Regional Water Quality Control Board ("Water Board"), under
certain specified conditions relating to clean up of contamination at the subject property. The
applicant has requested the Water Board approve a remedial action plan guiding the remediation
phase of the project. This action is a precursor to petitioning the Water Board to modify or
terminate the restrictive covenant prohibiting residential use of the property. Any modification or
termination would be based on post-remediation soil testing. The applicant intends to petition the
Water Board to modify the deed restriction.

Neither the subject property nor the proposed project can be viewed from your property as
there are numerous intervening developments and topography which block the view of the subject
property and proposed project from your property.

An initial study/mitigated negative declaration was prepared for the proposed project and
was circulated for public comment. Comment regarding the initial study and mitigated negative
declaration was received from interested agencies and persons on July 1, 2015, and July 31, 2015.
The City consulted with the Planning Commission and undertook revising the initial study to

File No. A-17-057
Page No. 3

address the comments made by the Novato Unified School District and the public. The revised
initial study has been completed and shows all potentially significant impacts can be mitigated to a
less than significant level, including impacts relating to hazardous materials, traffic, noise, and
aesthetics. An errata to the revised initial study has also been prepared to add a future potential
nearby development proposal at the North Bay Children's Center to the cumulative impacts analysis.
The addition of the nearby project to the CEQA analysis did not trigger any new or increased
significant cumulative impacts.

The Novato Charter school is a public charter school located at 940 C Street ("Charter
School"). A portion of the Novato Charter School property (athletic fields) is 50-feet from the
subject property. Parents of children at the Charter School and the Novato Unified School District
have raised concerns about the potential exposure of children to soil contaminants during
remediation and the general intensification of development near the Charter School.

The North Bay Children's Center is a non-profit daycare/preschool facility located at 932 C
Street. The facility is 50-feet from the subject property. Comments regarding the proposed project
or its CEQA document have not, at this time, been submitted by the North Bay Children's Center.
Your children attend Novato Charter School and the North Bay Children's Center. Your spouse
works at the Novato Charter School and earns approximately $60,000 annually from this
employment.

The proposed project will be considered by the Novato City Council in stages. The first
stage will be the remediation phase consisting of the possible adoption of the CEQA mitigated
negative declaration and issuance of a use permit to allow the remediation work to proceed. In the
event that the deed restriction is thereafter released by the Water Board, the City Council will then
be called upon to consider the balance of the project entitlements to construct the proposed
condominium project.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental
decision within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental
decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the public official's "interests" as
specified in Section 87103. Interests pertinent to your request for advice are:

• A public official has an interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect
interest² of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b).) This would include your interest in your
residence.

_______________
² An indirect investment or interest means any investment or interest owned by the spouse of an official or by a
member of the official's immediate family, by an agent on behalf of a public official, or by a business entity or trust in
which the official, the official's immediate family, or their agents own directly, indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent
interest or greater. (Section 87103.)

File No. A-17-057
Page No. 4

• A public official has an interest in any source of income, including promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).) However, Section 82030(b)(2) provides that income does not include: "Salary and reimbursement for expenses or per diem . . . received from a state, local, or federal government agency . . . ." (Section 82030(b)(2).) Since wages received by your spouse from the school are excluded from the definition of income, you do not have an interest in the school for purposes of Section 87100 and Section 87103(c).

• A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those of his or her immediate family -- this is the "personal financial effects" rule.³ (Section 87103.) You have provided no facts to suggest that the decision in question will affect the amount of money your spouse is paid by the school. Moreover, Regulation 18702.5(c) provides that if a decision affects . . . real property in which the official has an interest, materiality is determined pursuant to . . . Regulation 18702.2, relating to real property interests. Therefore, we do not further analyze personal finances.

The only remaining interest for our consideration is your interest in real property. For a conflict of interest to exist with respect to your real property, a governmental decision must have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the property.

Foreseeability

The standard for foreseeability differs depending on whether or not an interest is explicitly involved in the decision. An interest is explicitly involved, and the financial effect is generally presumed to be reasonably foreseeable, where the interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the official or the official's agency or the decision otherwise affects the official's real property as described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(1)-(6). (Regulation 18701(a).) If the interest is "not explicitly involved" in the decision, as here, then the financial effect is reasonably foreseeable if the effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical.

Materiality

Regulation 18702.2 provides materiality standards applicable to real property. Pertinent to your facts, Regulation 18702.2(a) provides that an effect will be material if the decision:

• Would change the character of the parcel of real property by substantially altering traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking, of property surrounding the official's real property parcel, the view, privacy, noise levels, or air quality, including odors, or any other factors that would affect the market value of the real property parcel in which the official has a financial interest.

³ Regulation 18702.5(c) provides that if a decision affects a business entity or real property in which the official has an interest, materiality is determined pursuant to Regulation 18702.1, relating to business entity interests, or Regulation 18702.2, relating to real property interests. Because the decisions at issue affect your real-property interest, we apply the materiality standards applicable to property, and we do not further address your interest in your personal finances.

File No. A-17-057
Page No. 5


• Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and consideration under the circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision was of such a nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the market value of the official's property.

Your property is 2,400 feet away from the proposed 31-unit residential condominium project. You noted that neither the subject property nor the proposed project can be viewed from your property as there are numerous intervening developments and that block the view of the subject property and proposed project. Additionally, the initial study/mitigated negative declaration has been completed and shows all potentially significant impacts can be mitigated to a less than significant level, including impacts relating to hazardous materials, traffic, noise, and aesthetics. Therefore, we conclude that the decision will not have a foreseeable material financial effect on your interests.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Assistant General Counsel
Legal Division

JWW:jgl

sections.analysis:
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or 
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the 
official has a financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental 
decision within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental 
decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the public official's "interests" as 
specified in Section 87103. Interests pertinent to your request for advice are: 
• A public official has an interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect 
interest2 of$2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b).) This would include your interest in your 
residence. 
2 An indirect investment or interest means any investment or interest owned by the spouse of an official or by a 
member of the official's immediate family, by an agent on behalf of a public official, or by a business entity or trust in 
which the official, the official's immediate family, or their agents own directly, indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent 
interest or greater. (Section 87103.) 


File No. A -17-057  
• A public official has an interest in any source of income, including promised income, which 
aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).) 
However, Section 82 03 0(b )( 2) provides that income does not include: "Salary and 
reimbursement for expenses or per diem ... received from a state, local, or federal 
government agency .... " (Section 82 03 0(b)( 2).) Since wages received by your spouse from 
the school are excluded from the definition of income, you do not have an interest in the 
school for purposes of Section 87100 and Section 87103(c). 
• A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those of 
his or her immediate family --this is the "personal financial effects" rule.3 (Section 87103.) 
You have provided no facts to suggest that the decision in question will affect the amount of 
money your spouse is paid by the school. Moreover, Regulation 18702.S(c) provides that if 
a decision affects ... real property in which the official has an interest, materiality is 
determined pursuant to . .. Regulation 18702.2, relating to real property interests. Therefore, 
we do not further analyze personal finances. 
The only remaining interest for our consideration is your interest in real property. For a 
conflict of interest to exist with respect to your real property, a governmental decision must have a 
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the property. 
Foreseeability 
The standard for foreseeability differs depending on whether or not an interest is explicitly 
involved in the decision. An interest is explicitly involved, and the financial effect is generally 
presumed to be reasonably foreseeable, where the interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a 
governmental decision before the official or the official's agency or the decision otherwise affects 
the official's real property as described in Regulation I 8702.2(a)(l ) -(6). (Regulation 18701 (a).) If 
the interest is "not explicitly involved" in the decision, as here, then the financial effect is 
reasonably foreseeable if the effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than 
hypothetical or theoretical. 
Materiality 
Regulation 18702.2 provides materiality standards applicable to real property. Pertinent to 
your facts, Regulation 18702.2(a) provides that an effect will be material if the decision: 
• Would change the character of the parcel of real property by substantially altering traffic 
levels or intensity of use, including parking, of property surrounding the official's real 
property parcel, the view, privacy, noise levels, or air quality, including odors, or any other 
factors that would affect the market value of the real property parcel in which the official 
has a financial interest. 
3 Regulation 18702.S(c) provides that if a decision affects a business entity or real property in which the 
official has an interest, materiality is determined pursuant to Regulation 18702.1, relating to business entity interests, or 
Regulation 18702.2, relating to real property interests. Because the decisions at issue affect your real-property interest, 
we apply the materiality standards applicable to property, and we do not further address your interest in your personal 
finances.  
• Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and consideration under the 
circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision was of such a nature that its 
reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the market value of the official's property. 
Your property is 2,400 feet away from the proposed 31-unit residential condominium 
project. You noted that neither the subject property nor the proposed project can be viewed from 
your property as there are numerous intervening developments and that block the view of the 
subject property and proposed project. Additionally, the initial study/mitigated negative declaration

significant level, including impacts relating to hazardous materials, traffic, noise, and aesthetics.

approximately 2 ,400 feet from the Hamilton Square 31-unit residential condominium project (the 
"project") or based on the income of your spouse from Novato Charter School which may be 
affected by the proposed project?

CONCLUSION: You do not have a conflict of interest in decision regarding the project. The decisions will 
not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your property (as discussed below).
